metadata.title: Topology Knowledge Graph Dataset
metadata.description: Topology content nodes with enriched statements/explanations and first-class directed dependency/provenance edges, updated from topology_.json.

fields.id: Stable slug identifier for the node.
fields.kind: Mathematical content type such as theorem, definition, lemma, proposition, corollary, example, exercise, or remark.
fields.number: Displayed item number from the source text.
fields.title: Human-readable title.
fields.chapter: Source chapter label; numeric chapters and single-letter appendix labels are supported.
fields.section: Source section label; numeric sections and single-letter appendix labels are supported.
fields.originalText: Cleaned source text, with Unicode math converted where possible to MathJax/LaTeX.
fields.statementDependencies: IDs of nodes needed to understand the statement.
fields.proofDependencies: IDs of nodes used in the proof or justification.
fields.edge.label: Human-readable edge relation.
fields.qualityFlags: Review or quality annotations for a node.
fields.mergedFrom: Source fragment(s) merged into this node during cleanup.
fields.edge.rationale: Human-readable reason for the directed edge.
fields.edge.review: Review flags and human-review status for the edge.
fields.edge.semanticRelation: Mathematical/provenance relation within the app-compatible relation enum.
fields.mathematicalFormula: Optional concise KaTeX-ready formula string, stored without outer math delimiters.

changes: removed duplicate proof-continuation node extracted as theorem 3.16
merged the removed text into Theorem 3.23 as a mergedFrom fragment
filled previously blank formalStatement and explanation fields with reviewable drafts
made node dependency arrays consistent with canonical edge directions
added rationales, labels, and review metadata to edges
added cleanText, statementText, labels, chapter groups, and quality flags to nodes

dependencyPass.name: mvp-mathematical-dependencies
dependencyPass.scope: conservative MVP: remove unreviewed auto edges, retain verified edges, add curated topological backbone, recompute dependency arrays

provenancePass.name: mvp-mathematical-provenance
provenancePass.purpose: Add high-confidence mathematical provenance edges while preserving the app-compatible relation enum.
provenancePass.edgeDirection: from = mathematical source/prerequisite; to = dependent concept/result/example
remainingMvpLimitations: This is not yet a full proof audit of every theorem.
Many examples still only have prerequisite-style edges, not all possible example-of/application-of provenance edges.
Appendix algebra/set-theory nodes were left mostly as foundations rather than heavily interconnected.

formulaPass.name: definition-mathematical-formulas
formulaPass.scope: Added concise KaTeX-ready mathematicalFormula strings to definition nodes where helpful for display.
formulaPass.formulaStyle: raw KaTeX without outer delimiters; render with asDisplayMath(...) in the UI

theoremFormulaPass.name: add-theorem-mathematical-formulas
theoremFormulaPass.scope: Added compact raw KaTeX mathematicalFormula strings for all theorem nodes where a formula is useful for rendering.

formulaEdgeAudit.name: formula-edge-audit
formulaEdgeAudit.scope: Audited dependencies introduced or made explicit by mathematicalFormula fields for touched definition and theorem nodes.
formulaEdgeAudit.policy: Conservative: add only edges whose source concept is explicitly used by the displayed formula or theorem statement.

nodes.title: Brouwer Fixed Point Theorem
nodes.originalText:
Let рЭСУ вИґ рЭРЈрЭСЫ вЖТ рЭРЈрЭСЫ be a continuous map from the (unit) disk in вДЭрЭСЫ to itself. Then рЭСУ has a fixed point, i.e., there is some point рЭС• вИИ рЭРЈрЭСЫ such that рЭСУ (рЭС•) = рЭС•.

For рЭСЫ = 1 this is a well-known result from calculus: The graph of any continuous map рЭСУ вИґ [0, 1] вЖТ [0, 1] must cross the diagonal рЭС¶ = рЭС• for some рЭС•вИЧ вИИ [0, 1]. Hence, рЭСУ (рЭС•вИЧ ) = рЭС•вИЧ . See Figure 1.4. The second theorem is the fundamental theorem of algebra.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $f : D^n \to D^n$ be a continuous map from the unit disk in $\mathbb{R}^n$ to itself. Then there exists $x \in D^n$ such that $f(x)=x$.
nodes.explanation: The theorem asserts that every continuous self-map of the closed unit disk has a fixed point. In the introduction it is used as a motivating example: in dimension one, the graph of a continuous map $[0,1]\to[0,1]$ must cross the diagonal.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: f:D^n\to D^n\text{ continuous }\Longrightarrow \exists x\in D^n:\ f(x)=x

nodes.title: Fundamental Theorem of Algebra
nodes.originalText:
A polynomial equation

рЭСІрЭСЫ + рЭСОрЭСЫвИТ1 рЭСІрЭСЫвИТ1 + вЛѓ + рЭСО1 рЭСІ + рЭСО0 = 0

of degree рЭСЫ > 0 with complex coefficients has at least one complex root.

Chapter 1. Introduction 7

рЭС¶ (1, 1) вИЩ

рЭС¶=рЭС• рЭС¶ = рЭСУ (рЭС•) рЭС•

рЭС•вИЧ рЭС•

Figure 1.4: The graph of any continuous map from [0, 1] to [0, 1] must cross the diagonal.

To prove it we will use the fact that the fundamental group of the circle is isomorphic to the group of integers. The fundamental theorem of algebra may be proved in many different ways, including using only algebraic techniques and analysis. However, the proof we will provide (based on [4, ¬І56]) is a fairly simple corollary of the computation of the fundamental group of the circle.

2. Continuous maps

2.1 Metric spaces From calculus we know what to mean by a continuous map from вДЭрЭСЫ to вДЭрЭСЪ : a map рЭСУ вИґ вДЭрЭСЫ вЖТ вДЭрЭСЪ is continuous at рЭСЭ вИИ вДЭрЭСЫ if for all рЭЬЦ > 0 there exists a рЭЫњ > 0 such that if вАЦрЭСЭ вИТ рЭСЮвАЦвДЭрЭСЫ < рЭЫњ, then вАЦрЭСУ (рЭСЭ) вИТ рЭСУ (рЭСЮ)вАЦвДЭрЭСЪ < рЭЬЦ. Here вАЦ вЛЕ вАЦвДЭрЭСЫ denotes the Euclidean norm in вДЭрЭСЫ . Similarly, вАЦ вЛЕ вАЦвДЭрЭСЪ denotes the Euclidean norm in вДЭрЭСЪ . Topological spaces provide the most general setting for which the concept of continuity makes sense. Before we get to the concept of a topological space, let us consider metric spaces. Metric spaces allow us to speak of distance between elements. Using the notion of distance between elements we can make sense of continuity of maps between metric spaces.
nodes.formalStatement: Every nonconstant polynomial with complex coefficients has at least one complex root. Equivalently, every polynomial equation $z^n+a_{n-1}z^{n-1}+\cdots+a_1z+a_0=0$ with $n>0$ has a solution in $\mathbb{C}$.
nodes.explanation: This introductory theorem is presented as a later application of topology: the text notes that it will be proved using the computation of the fundamental group of the circle.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: p(z)=z^n+a_{n-1}z^{n-1}+\cdots+a_1z+a_0,\ n>0\Longrightarrow \exists z\in\mathbb{C}:p(z)=0

nodes.title: Metric spaces
nodes.originalText:
A metric space (рЭСЛ, рЭСС) is a non-empty set рЭСЛ together with a map рЭСС вИґ рЭСЛ √Ч рЭСЛ вЖТ вДЭ called a metric such that the following properties hold:

M1 рЭСС(рЭС•, рЭС¶) в©Њ 0 for all рЭС•, рЭС¶ вИИ рЭСЛ, and рЭСС(рЭС•, рЭС¶) = 0 if and only if рЭС• = рЭС¶;

M2 рЭСС(рЭС•, рЭС¶) = рЭСС(рЭС¶, рЭС•) for all рЭС•, рЭС¶ вИИ рЭСЛ;

M3 рЭСС(рЭС•, рЭСІ) в©љ рЭСС(рЭС•, рЭС¶) + рЭСС(рЭС¶, рЭСІ) for all рЭС•, рЭС¶, рЭСІ вИИ рЭСЛ.

The first condition says that the distance between two elements is always positive, and equal to zero if and only if the two elements are the same. The second condition says that distance is symmetric. The third condition says that the triangle inequality holds. The metric рЭСС is sometimes also referred to as a distance function.

2.2 (вДЭрЭСЫ seen as a metric space) Let рЭСЛ = вДЭ and рЭСС be the map defined by рЭСС(рЭС•, рЭС¶) = Example вИЪ |рЭС• вИТ рЭС¶|(= (рЭС• вИТ рЭС¶)2 ). The first two requirements for рЭСС are clearly satisfied, and the third follows from the usual triangle inequality for real numbers,

рЭСС(рЭС•, рЭСІ) = |рЭС• вИТ рЭСІ| = |(рЭС• вИТ рЭС¶) + (рЭС¶ вИТ рЭСІ)| в©љ |рЭС• вИТ рЭС¶| + |рЭС¶ вИТ рЭСІ| = рЭСС(рЭС•, рЭС¶) + рЭСС(рЭС¶, рЭСІ).

For рЭСЛ = вДЭрЭСЫ with рЭСЫ > 0 an integer, вИЪ let рЭСС(рЭС•, рЭС¶) = вАЦрЭС• вИТ рЭС¶вАЦ where вАЦ вЛЕ вАЦ is the Euclidean norm, e.g., for рЭСЫ = 2, рЭСС(рЭС•, рЭС¶) = вАЦрЭС• вИТ рЭС¶вАЦ = (рЭС•1 вИТ рЭС¶1 )2 + (рЭС•2 вИТ рЭС¶2 )2 . Again, the first two requirements for рЭСС are clearly satisfied. The third requirement follows from the triangle inequality for vectors in вДЭрЭСЫ .

We may equip вДЭрЭСЫ with other metrics than the one described in Example 2.2. For instance, for рЭСЛ = вДЭ2 , let рЭСС(рЭС•, рЭС¶) = |рЭС•1 вИТ рЭС¶1 | + |рЭС•2 вИТ рЭС¶2 |.

This is known as the taxi cab metric.

Chapter 2. Continuous maps 9

We say that two metrics рЭСС1 and рЭСС2 on the same set рЭСЛ are equivalent if there are constants рЭРњ and рЭСА such that рЭСС1 (рЭС•, рЭС¶) в©љ рЭРњрЭСС2 (рЭС•, рЭС¶) and рЭСС2 (рЭС•, рЭС¶) в©љ рЭСАрЭСС1 (рЭС•, рЭС¶)

for all рЭС•, рЭС¶ вИИ рЭСЛ.
nodes.formalStatement: A metric space is a pair $(X,d)$ where $X$ is a nonempty set and $d:X\times X\to\mathbb{R}$ satisfies, for all $x,y,z\in X$: $d(x,y)\geq 0$ with equality if and only if $x=y$; $d(x,y)=d(y,x)$; and $d(x,z)\leq d(x,y)+d(y,z)$.
nodes.explanation: A metric space is a set equipped with a distance function. The three axioms give positivity, symmetry, and the triangle inequality, making metric spaces the starting point for continuity and open-set definitions.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: d:X\times X\to\mathbb{R},\quad d(x,y)\ge 0,\quad d(x,y)=0\iff x=y,\quad d(x,y)=d(y,x),\quad d(x,z)\le d(x,y)+d(y,z)

nodes.title: Discrete metric spaces
nodes.originalText: For any set рЭСЛ, let рЭСС вИґ рЭСЛ √Ч рЭСЛ вЖТ вДЭ be the map given by { 1 рЭС• вЙ† рЭС¶, рЭСС(рЭС•, рЭС¶) = 0 рЭС• = рЭС¶. We call рЭСС the discrete metric on рЭСЛ.
nodes.formalStatement: For any set $X$, define $d:X\times X\to\mathbb{R}$ by $d(x,y)=0$ if $x=y$ and $d(x,y)=1$ if $x\neq y$. This is the discrete metric on $X$.
nodes.explanation: The discrete metric gives every distinct pair of points distance one. It is the simplest example of a metric space and later makes open balls especially easy to compute.

nodes.title: C[a,b] with the Sup Metric
nodes.originalText: Let рЭСЛ = рЭРґ[рЭСО, рЭСП], i.e., the set of continuous maps from the interval рЭРЉ = [рЭСО, рЭСП] вКЖ вДЭ to вДЭ, and let рЭСС(рЭС•, рЭС¶) = max |рЭС•(рЭСЦ) вИТ рЭС¶(рЭСЦ)|. рЭСЦвИИрЭРЉ
nodes.formalStatement: Let $X=C[a,b]$ be the set of continuous maps from $[a,b]\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ to $\mathbb{R}$. The function $d(x,y)=\max_{i\in[a,b]} |x(i)-y(i)|$ defines a metric on $X$.
nodes.explanation: This example puts a metric on a function space using maximum pointwise distance. It shows that metric spaces need not consist of points in Euclidean space.

nodes.title: Restricted Metric on a Subset
nodes.originalText:
If рЭСС is a metric on a set рЭСЛ, and рЭРі вКЖ рЭСЛ is any subset of рЭСЛ, then рЭСС is also a metric on рЭРі.

2.2 Continuous maps between metric spaces The definition of continuity of maps between metric spaces is completely analogous to the situation that we have from calculus.
nodes.formalStatement: If $d$ is a metric on a set $X$ and $A\subseteq X$, then the restriction of $d$ to $A\times A$ is a metric on $A$.
nodes.explanation: A metric can be restricted to any subset of its space. This is the metric-space precursor to later subspace constructions.

nodes.title: Continuous maps between metric spaces
nodes.originalText:
Let (рЭСЛ, рЭССрЭСЛ ) and (рЭСМ , рЭССрЭСМ ) be two metric spaces. A map рЭСУ вИґ рЭСЛ вЖТ рЭСМ is continuous at рЭСЭ вИИ рЭСЛ if for all рЭЬЦ > 0 there is a рЭЫњ > 0 such that if рЭССрЭСЛ (рЭСЭ, рЭСЮ) < рЭЫњ then рЭССрЭСМ (рЭСУ (рЭСЭ), рЭСУ (рЭСЮ)) < рЭЬЦ. If рЭСУ is continuous at every рЭСЭ вИИ рЭСЛ, we say that рЭСУ is continuous.

To get us to the setting of topological spaces we will need the concept of open and closed sets.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $(X,d_X)$ and $(Y,d_Y)$ be metric spaces. A map $f:X\to Y$ is continuous at $p\in X$ if for every $\epsilon>0$ there exists $\delta>0$ such that $d_X(p,q)<\delta$ implies $d_Y(f(p),f(q))<\epsilon$. The map $f$ is continuous if it is continuous at every point of $X$.
nodes.explanation: Continuity between metric spaces is the epsilon-delta condition expressed using the two metrics. This definition is the metric version that will later be reformulated using open sets.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: f\text{ continuous at }p\iff \forall \varepsilon>0\ \exists \delta>0\ \forall q\in X,\ d_X(p,q)<\delta\Rightarrow d_Y(f(p),f(q))<\varepsilon

nodes.title: Open and closed balls
nodes.originalText:
Let (рЭСЛ, рЭСС) be a metric space, and let рЭСО вИИ рЭСЛ and рЭСЯ > 0 be real number. The open ball centered at рЭСО with radius рЭСЯ is the subset

рЭЦ°(рЭСО; рЭСЯ) = {рЭС• вИИ рЭСЛ вИ£ рЭСС(рЭС•, рЭСО) < рЭСЯ}

of рЭСЛ. The closed ball centered at рЭСО with radius рЭСЯ is the subset

рЭЦ°(рЭСО; рЭСЯ) = {рЭС• вИИ рЭСЛ вИ£ рЭСС(рЭС•, рЭСО) в©љ рЭСЯ}

of рЭСЛ.

In Euclidean space with the usual metric (induced from Euclidean norm), a ball (as defined above) is precisely what we think of as a ball in everyday language. Open balls are sometimes referred to as simply balls, and closed balls are sometimes referred to as discs, e.g. Theorem 1.1.

10 2.2. Continuous maps between metric spaces
nodes.formalStatement: Let $(X,d)$ be a metric space, let $a\in X$, and let $r>0$. The open ball centered at $a$ with radius $r$ is $B(a;r)=\{x\in X\mid d(x,a)<r\}$, and the closed ball centered at $a$ with radius $r$ is $\overline{B}(a;r)=\{x\in X\mid d(x,a)\leq r\}$.
nodes.explanation: Open and closed balls are the basic local neighborhoods determined by a metric. They are used to define open sets and to express continuity at a point.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: B(a;r)=\{x\in X\mid d(x,a)<r\},\qquad \overline{B}(a;r)=\{x\in X\mid d(x,a)\le r\}

nodes.title: Open balls in discrete metric spaces
nodes.originalText: Let (рЭСЛ, рЭСС) be the metric space defined in Example 2.3. Then рЭЦ°(рЭС•; рЭСЯ1 ) = {рЭС•} and рЭЦ°(рЭС•; рЭСЯ2 ) = рЭСЛ for all 0 < рЭСЯ1 в©љ 1 and all рЭСЯ2 > 1.
nodes.formalStatement: In a discrete metric space $(X,d)$, $B(x;r)=\{x\}$ for $0<r\leq 1$, while $B(x;r)=X$ for $r>1$.
nodes.explanation: This example computes metric balls in the discrete metric. It shows how the radius controls whether an open ball isolates one point or contains the whole space.

nodes.title: Open and closed sets
nodes.originalText:
Let (рЭСЛ, рЭСС) be a metric space. A subset рЭРі вКЖ рЭСЛ is open in рЭСЛ if for every point рЭСО вИИ рЭРі, there is an open ball рЭЦ°(рЭСО; рЭСЯ) about рЭСО contained in рЭРі. We say that рЭРі is closed in рЭСЛ if the complement рЭРірЭСР = рЭСЛ вІµ рЭРі = {рЭС• вИИ рЭСЛ вИ£ рЭС• вИЙ рЭРі} is open.

Most subsets are neither open nor closed. Subsets that are both open and closed are sometimes referred to as clopen. In particular, both вИЕ and рЭСЛ are clopen in рЭСЛ.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $(X,d)$ be a metric space. A subset $A\subseteq X$ is open in $X$ if for every $a\in A$ there exists an open ball $B(a;r)$ with $B(a;r)\subseteq A$. The subset $A$ is closed in $X$ if its complement $A^c=X\setminus A$ is open.
nodes.explanation: Open sets are subsets containing a metric ball around each of their points. Closed sets are defined by open complements, and this language prepares the transition from metric spaces to topological spaces.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: A\text{ open}\iff \forall a\in A\ \exists r>0\text{ such that }B(a;r)\subseteq A,\qquad A\text{ closed}\iff X\setminus A\text{ is open}

nodes.title: Metric Balls Are Open or Closed
nodes.originalText:
Let (рЭСЛ, рЭСС) be a metric space, рЭС• вИИ рЭСЛ and рЭСЯ > 0 a real number. Then the open ball рЭЦ°(рЭС•; рЭСЯ) вКЖ рЭСЛ is open in рЭСЛ, and the closed ball рЭЦ°(рЭС•; рЭСЯ) вКЖ рЭСЛ is closed in рЭСЛ.

Proof. We prove the statement about open balls. The statement about closed balls follows from a similar argument. Assume that рЭС¶ вИИ рЭЦ°(рЭС•; рЭСЯ). We need to prove that there is an open ball рЭЦ°(рЭС¶; рЭЬЦ) about рЭС¶ that is contained in рЭЦ°(рЭС•; рЭСЯ). Let рЭЬЦ = рЭСЯ вИТ рЭСС(рЭС•, рЭС¶). By the triangle inequality of the metric рЭСС, M3, we have that for рЭСІ вИИ рЭЦ°(рЭС¶; рЭЬЦ),

рЭСС(рЭС•, рЭСІ) в©љ рЭСС(рЭС•, рЭС¶) + рЭСС(рЭС¶, рЭСІ) < рЭСС(рЭС•, рЭС¶) + рЭЬЦ = рЭСС(рЭС•, рЭС¶) + рЭСЯ вИТ рЭСС(рЭС•, рЭС¶) = рЭСЯ.

Hence, рЭЦ°(рЭС¶; рЭЬЦ) вКЖ рЭЦ°(рЭС•; рЭСЯ).

For a metric space (рЭСЛ, рЭСС), a subset рЭРі вКЖ рЭСЛ and рЭС• вИИ рЭСЛ, we say that: (i) рЭС• is an interior point of рЭРі if there is an open ball рЭЦ°(рЭС•; рЭСЯ) about рЭС• which is contained in рЭРі, (ii) рЭС• is an exterior point of рЭРі if there is an open ball рЭЦ°(рЭС•; рЭСЯ) which is contained in рЭРірЭСР and (iii) рЭС• is a boundary point if all open balls about рЭС• contains points in рЭРі and in рЭРірЭСР . Hence, рЭРі is open in рЭСЛ if and only if рЭРі only consists of its interior points. An interior point will always belong to рЭРі. An exterior point will never belong to рЭРі. A boundary point will some times belong to рЭРі, and some times to рЭРірЭСР .
nodes.formalStatement: Let $(X,d)$ be a metric space, let $x\in X$, and let $r>0$. Then the open ball $B(x;r)$ is open in $X$, and the closed ball $\overline{B}(x;r)$ is closed in $X$.
nodes.explanation: This lemma verifies that the basic metric balls behave as their names suggest. The proof for open balls uses the triangle inequality to fit a smaller ball around any chosen point.

nodes.title: Neighborhoods
nodes.originalText: Let (рЭСЛ, рЭСС) be a metric space, рЭРі a subset of рЭСЛ and рЭС• вИИ рЭСЛ. We say that рЭРі is a neighborhood of рЭС• if there is an open ball about рЭС• that is contained in рЭРі. We say that рЭРі is an open neighborhood (of рЭС•) if рЭРі itself is open.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $(X,d)$ be a metric space, let $A\subseteq X$, and let $x\in X$. The set $A$ is a neighborhood of $x$ if there is an open ball about $x$ contained in $A$. It is an open neighborhood of $x$ if $A$ itself is open.
nodes.explanation: A neighborhood records the idea that a set contains some open ball around a point. This gives a pointwise way to discuss local behavior in metric spaces.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: A\text{ is a neighborhood of }x\iff \exists r>0\text{ such that }B(x;r)\subseteq A

nodes.title: Continuity at a point
nodes.originalText:
Let (рЭСЛ, рЭССрЭСЛ ) and (рЭСМ , рЭССрЭСМ ) be two metric spaces, and let рЭСЭ вИИ рЭСЛ. A map рЭСУ вИґ рЭСЛ вЖТ рЭСМ is continuous at рЭСЭ if and only if for all neighborhoods рЭРµ of рЭСУ (рЭСЭ), there is a neighborhood рЭРі of рЭСЭ such that рЭСУ (рЭРі) вКЖ рЭРµ.

Chapter 2. Continuous maps 11

Proof. Assume that рЭСУ is continuous at рЭСЭ. If рЭРµ is a neighborhood of рЭСУ (рЭСЭ), then, by definition, there is an open ball рЭЦ°рЭСМ (рЭСУ (рЭСЭ); рЭЬЦ) about рЭСУ (рЭСЭ) that is contained in рЭРµ. Since рЭСУ is continuous at рЭСЭ, there is a рЭЫњ > 0 such that if рЭССрЭСЛ (рЭСЭ, рЭСЮ) < рЭЫњ, then рЭССрЭСМ (рЭСУ (рЭСЭ), рЭСУ (рЭСЮ)) < рЭЬЦ. Hence, рЭСУ (рЭЦ°рЭСЛ (рЭСЭ; рЭЫњ)) вКЖ рЭЦ°рЭСМ (рЭСУ (рЭСЭ); рЭЬЦ) вКЖ рЭРµ. That is, if we let рЭРі = рЭЦ°рЭСЛ (рЭСЭ, рЭЫњ), then for all neighborhoods рЭРµ of рЭСУ (рЭСЭ), we have that рЭСУ (рЭРі) вКЖ рЭРµ where рЭРі is a neighborhood of рЭСЭ. Assume that for all neighborhoods рЭРµ of рЭСУ (рЭСЭ), there is a neighborhood рЭРі of рЭСЭ such that рЭСУ (рЭРі) вКЖ рЭРµ. We need to prove that for all рЭЬЦ > 0, there is a рЭЫњ > 0 such that if рЭССрЭСЛ (рЭСЭ, рЭСЮ) < рЭЫњ, then рЭССрЭСМ (рЭСУ (рЭСЭ), рЭСУ (рЭСЮ)) < рЭЬЦ. By utilizing the fact that рЭРµ = рЭЦ°рЭСМ (рЭСУ (рЭСЭ); рЭЬЦ) is a neighborhood of рЭСУ (рЭСЭ), then, by assumption, there must be a neighborhood рЭРі of рЭСЭ such that рЭСУ (рЭРі) вКЖ рЭРµ. Since рЭРі is a neighborhood of рЭСЭ, there is an open ball рЭЦ°рЭСЛ (рЭСЭ; рЭЫњ) about рЭСЭ that is contained in рЭРі. Now assume that рЭССрЭСЛ (рЭСЭ, рЭСЭвА≤ ) < рЭЫњ. Then рЭСЭвА≤ вИИ рЭЦ°рЭСЛ (рЭСЭ; рЭЫњ) вКЖ рЭРі. Thus рЭСУ (рЭСЭвА≤ ) вИИ рЭРµ = рЭЦ°рЭСМ (рЭСУ (рЭСЭ); рЭЬЦ), and hence, рЭССрЭСМ (рЭСУ (рЭСЭ), рЭСУ (рЭСЭвА≤ )) < рЭЬЦ. Thus рЭСУ is continuous at рЭСЭ.

The following theorem gives an alternative description of continuous maps between metric spaces.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $(X,d_X)$ and $(Y,d_Y)$ be metric spaces, let $p\in X$, and let $f:X\to Y$. Then $f$ is continuous at $p$ if and only if for every neighborhood $B$ of $f(p)$ there exists a neighborhood $A$ of $p$ such that $f(A)\subseteq B$.
nodes.explanation: This theorem recasts epsilon-delta continuity using neighborhoods. The proof translates neighborhoods into open balls and then applies the metric definition of continuity.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: f\text{ continuous at }p\iff \forall B\in\mathcal{N}(f(p)),\ \exists A\in\mathcal{N}(p):\ f(A)\subseteq B

nodes.title: Preimage Criterion for Metric Continuity
nodes.originalText:
Let (рЭСЛ, рЭССрЭСЛ ) and (рЭСМ , рЭССрЭСМ ) be two metric spaces. A map рЭСУ вИґ рЭСЛ вЖТ рЭСМ is continuous if and only if for every subset рЭРµ вКЖ рЭСМ open in рЭСМ , the preimage of рЭРµ under рЭСУ ,

рЭСУ вИТ1 (рЭРµ) = {рЭС• вИИ рЭСЛ вИ£ рЭСУ (рЭС•) вИИ рЭРµ} вКЖ рЭСЛ,

is open in рЭСЛ.

Proof. Assume that рЭСУ is continuous. For рЭРµ вКЖ рЭСМ open in рЭСМ , we have to prove that рЭСУ вИТ1 (рЭРµ) вКЖ рЭСЛ is open in рЭСЛ. Let рЭСО вИИ рЭСУ вИТ1 (рЭРµ). We want to prove that there is an open ball about рЭСО in рЭСЛ that is contained in рЭСУ вИТ1 (рЭРµ). By assumption, рЭРµ вКЖ рЭСМ is open in рЭСМ . Hence, there is an рЭЬЦ > 0 such that рЭЦ°рЭСМ (рЭСУ (рЭСО); рЭЬЦ) вКЖ рЭРµ. From the assumption that рЭСУ is continuous there is a рЭЫњ > 0 such that рЭЦ°рЭСЛ (рЭСО; рЭЫњ) вКЖ рЭСУ вИТ1 (рЭЦ°рЭСМ (рЭСУ (рЭСО); рЭЬЦ)) вКЖ рЭСУ вИТ1 (рЭРµ). We now prove the opposite implication. Assume that for every subset рЭРµ вКЖ рЭСМ open in рЭСМ , the preimage рЭСУ вИТ1 (рЭРµ) of рЭРµ under рЭСУ is open in рЭСЛ. Let рЭСО вИИ рЭСЛ and рЭЬЦ > 0 be a real number. From the first assumption it follows that рЭСУ вИТ1 (рЭЦ°рЭСМ (рЭСУ (рЭСО); рЭЬЦ)) вКЖ рЭСЛ is open in рЭСЛ. As рЭСУ вИТ1 (рЭЦ°рЭСМ (рЭСУ (рЭСО); рЭЬЦ)) is open and contains рЭСО, there is a рЭЫњ > 0 such that рЭЦ°рЭСЛ (рЭСО; рЭЫњ) вКЖ рЭСУ вИТ1 (рЭЦ°рЭСМ (рЭСУ (рЭСО); рЭЬЦ)). Thus рЭС• вИИ рЭЦ°рЭСЛ (рЭСО; рЭЫњ) implies that рЭСУ (рЭС•) вИИ рЭЦ°рЭСМ (рЭСУ (рЭСО); рЭЬЦ). Hence, рЭСУ вИґ рЭСЛ вЖТ рЭСМ is continuous.

Let рЭРі and рЭРµ be sets, and let рЭСУ вИґ рЭРі вЖТ рЭРµ. Then рЭСУ вИТ1 (рЭРµ) will always exist even if there is no inverse map. In the cases where рЭСУ has an inverse there is no ambiguity. If рЭСИ and рЭСЙ are both subsets of рЭРµ then

рЭСУ вИТ1 (рЭСИ вИ™ рЭСЙ ) = рЭСУ вИТ1 (рЭСИ ) вИ™ рЭСУ вИТ1 (рЭСЙ ) and рЭСУ вИТ1 (рЭСИ вИ© рЭСЙ ) = рЭСУ вИТ1 (рЭСИ ) вИ© рЭСУ вИТ1 (рЭСЙ ),

and furthermore, if рЭСИ вКЖ рЭСЙ then рЭСУ вИТ1 (рЭСИ ) вКЖ рЭСУ вИТ1 (рЭСЙ ). Let рЭСИ вКЖ рЭРі and рЭСЙ вКЖ рЭРµ, then ( ) рЭСИ вКЖ рЭСУ вИТ1 (рЭСУ (рЭСИ )) and рЭСУ рЭСУ вИТ1 (рЭСЙ ) вКЖ рЭСЙ .

We also note that if рЭСИ is a subset of рЭРµ then

рЭСУ вИТ1 (рЭРµ вІµ рЭСИ ) = рЭСУ вИТ1 (рЭСИ рЭСР ) = (рЭСУ вИТ1 (рЭСИ ))рЭСР = рЭРі вІµ рЭСУ вИТ1 (рЭСИ ).

12 2.3. Exercises

2.3 Exercises

Exercise 2.1 Does рЭСС(рЭС•, рЭС¶) = (рЭС• вИТ рЭС¶)2 define a metric on рЭСЛ = вДЭ?

Exercise 2.2 Show that вДЭ2 equipped with the taxi cab metric is a metric space.

Exercise 2.3 Let (рЭСЛ, рЭСС) be a metric space. Show that the map рЭСС вА≤ вИґ рЭСЛ √Ч рЭСЛ вЖТ вДЭ given by

рЭСС(рЭС•, рЭС¶) рЭСС вА≤ (рЭС•, рЭС¶) = 1 + рЭСС(рЭС•, рЭС¶) is also a metric on рЭСЛ.

Exercise 2.4 Draw a picture of the open ball рЭЦ°((0, 0); 1) in the metric space (вДЭ2 , рЭСС) with

(a) рЭСС(рЭС•, рЭС¶) = рЭСС1 (рЭС•, рЭС¶) = |рЭС•1 вИТ рЭС¶1 | + |рЭС•2 вИТ рЭС¶2 |; вИЪ (b) рЭСС(рЭС•, рЭС¶) = рЭСС2 (рЭС•, рЭС¶) = (рЭС•1 вИТ рЭС¶1 )2 + (рЭС•2 вИТ рЭС¶2 )2 ;

(c) рЭСС(рЭС•, рЭС¶) = рЭССвИЮ (рЭС•, рЭС¶) = max{|рЭС•1 вИТ рЭС¶1 |, |рЭС•2 вИТ рЭС¶2 |}.

Exercise 2.5 Show that рЭСС1 , рЭСС2 and рЭССвИЮ (as defined in Exercise 2.4) are equivalent on рЭСЛ = вДЭ2 .

Exercise 2.6 Show that in a discrete metric space (рЭСЛ, рЭСС), cf. Example 2.3, every subset is both open and closed in рЭСЛ.

Exercise 2.7 Show that for equivalent metrics рЭСС and рЭСС вА≤ on the set рЭСЛ, the open sets are the same.

Exercise 2.8 Let (рЭСЛ, рЭССрЭСЛ ) and (рЭСМ , рЭССрЭСМ ) be metric spaces, and let рЭСУ вИґ рЭСЛ вЖТ рЭСМ be a map. Show that рЭСУ is continuous if and only if for every subset рЭРµ вКЖ рЭСМ closed in рЭСМ , the preimage рЭСУ вИТ1 (рЭРµ) is closed in рЭСЛ.

3. Topological spaces

3.1 Definition and examples Topological spaces are spaces constructed to support continuous maps. The definition is as follows.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $(X,d_X)$ and $(Y,d_Y)$ be metric spaces. A map $f:X\to Y$ is continuous if and only if for every subset $B\subseteq Y$ that is open in $Y$, the preimage $f^{-1}(B)=\{x\in X\mid f(x)\in B\}$ is open in $X$.
nodes.explanation: This theorem identifies continuity with preservation of open sets under preimage. It is the bridge from metric continuity to the topological definition of continuity.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: f:X\to Y\text{ continuous}\iff \forall B\subseteq Y,\ B\text{ open}\Rightarrow f^{-1}(B)\text{ open in }X

nodes.title: Topological spaces
nodes.originalText:
A topological space is a set рЭСЛ together with a collection оЙА of subsets of рЭСЛ that are called open in рЭСЛ, such that the following properties hold.

T1 The subsets вИЕ and рЭСЛ are in оЙА .

T2 The union of the elements of any subcollection of оЙА is in оЙА .

T3 The intersection of the elements of any finite subcollection of оЙА is in оЙА .

A topological space is strictly speaking an ordered pair (рЭСЛ, оЙА ). We refer to оЙА as the topology on рЭСЛ. We will often omit specific mention of оЙА if no confusion will arise.

The following theorem states that every metric space (рЭСЛ, рЭСС) is a topological space with the metric topology оЙАрЭСС on рЭСЛ.
nodes.formalStatement: A topological space is a pair $(X,\mathcal{T})$ where $X$ is a set and $\mathcal{T}$ is a collection of subsets of $X$ such that $\emptyset,X\in\mathcal{T}$, arbitrary unions of elements of $\mathcal{T}$ lie in $\mathcal{T}$, and finite intersections of elements of $\mathcal{T}$ lie in $\mathcal{T}$. The elements of $\mathcal{T}$ are called open sets.
nodes.explanation: A topology abstracts the open-set behavior seen in metric spaces. The three axioms make open sets stable under the union and finite-intersection operations used throughout the subject.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: \mathcal{T}\subseteq\mathcal{P}(X),\quad \emptyset,X\in\mathcal{T},\quad \{U_i\}_{i\in I}\subseteq\mathcal{T}\Rightarrow\bigcup_{i\in I}U_i\in\mathcal{T},\quad U_1,\ldots,U_n\in\mathcal{T}\Rightarrow\bigcap_{k=1}^n U_k\in\mathcal{T}

nodes.title: Metric spaces are topological spaces
nodes.originalText:
Let (рЭСЛ, рЭСС) be a metric space. Let оЙАрЭСС be the collection of subsets рЭСИ вКЖ рЭСЛ with the property that рЭСИ вИИ оЙАрЭСС if and only if for each рЭС• вИИ рЭСИ there is an рЭСЯ > 0 such that рЭЦ°(рЭС•; рЭСЯ) вКЖ рЭСИ . Then оЙАрЭСС defines a topology on рЭСЛ.

Proof. Clearly, вИЕ вИИ оЙАрЭСС . To show that рЭСЛ вИИ оЙАрЭСС , note that for any рЭС• вИИ рЭСЛ, рЭЦ°(рЭС•; 1) вКЖ рЭСЛ. Hence, рЭСЛ вИИ оЙАрЭСС . Thus T1 is satisfied. вЛГ Let {рЭСИрЭЬЖ }рЭЬЖвИИќЫ be any subcollection of оЙАрЭСС . We need to prove that рЭСЙ = рЭЬЖвИИќЫ рЭСИрЭЬЖ вИИ оЙАрЭСС . Let рЭС• вИИ рЭСЙ . вЛГ From рЭСЙ = рЭЬЖвИИќЫ рЭСИрЭЬЖ there is рЭЬЖ вИИ ќЫ such that рЭС• вИИ рЭСИрЭЬЖ . By the property of рЭСИрЭЬЖ satisfied by the рЭСИрЭЬЖ in оЙАрЭСС there is an рЭСЯ > 0 such that рЭЦ°(рЭС•; рЭСЯ) вКЖ рЭСИрЭЬЖ . Hence, рЭЦ°(рЭС•; рЭСЯ) вКЖ рЭСЙ . Thus рЭСЙ вИИ оЙАрЭСС , and T2 is satisfied. We prove that the intersection of the elements of any finite subcollection of оЙАрЭСС is also an element of оЙАрЭСС . Let рЭСИ1 , рЭСИ2 , вА¶ , рЭСИрЭСЫ вИИ оЙАрЭСС . We need to prove that рЭСИ1 вИ© рЭСИ2 вИ© вЛѓ вИ© рЭСИрЭСЫ вИИ оЙАрЭСС . Let рЭС• вИИ рЭСИ1 вИ© рЭСИ2 вИ© вЛѓ вИ© рЭСИрЭСЫ . Since рЭСИ1 вИ© рЭСИ2 вИ© вЛѓ вИ© рЭСИрЭСЫ вКЖ рЭСИрЭСЦ , we have that рЭС• вИИ рЭСИрЭСЦ for рЭСЦ = 1, 2, вА¶ , рЭСЫ. By the defining property of оЙАрЭСС there is an рЭСЯрЭСЦ > 0 such that рЭЦ°(рЭС•; рЭСЯрЭСЦ ) вКЖ рЭСИрЭСЦ for рЭСЦ = 1, 2, вА¶ , рЭСЫ. Let рЭСЯ = min{рЭСЯ1 , рЭСЯ2 , вА¶ , рЭСЯрЭСЫ }. Then рЭЦ°(рЭС•; рЭСЯ) вКЖ рЭЦ°(рЭС•; рЭСЯрЭСЦ ) вКЖ рЭСИрЭСЦ for рЭСЦ = 1, 2, вА¶ , рЭСЫ. Thus рЭЦ°(рЭС•; рЭСЯ) вКЖ рЭСИ1 вИ©рЭСИ2 вИ©вЛѓвИ©рЭСИрЭСЫ , and so рЭСИ1 вИ©рЭСИ2 вИ©вЛѓвИ©рЭСИрЭСЫ вИИ оЙАрЭСС . Hence, T3 is satisfied.

The following theorem relates the metric topologies for two equivalent metrics.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $(X,d)$ be a metric space. Let $\mathcal{T}_d$ be the collection of subsets $U\subseteq X$ such that for each $x\in U$ there exists $r>0$ with $B(x;r)\subseteq U$. Then $\mathcal{T}_d$ is a topology on $X$.
nodes.explanation: Every metric determines a topology by declaring the metric-open subsets to be open. The proof checks the topology axioms using open balls and the metric definition of open sets.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: \mathcal{T}_d=\{U\subseteq X\mid \forall x\in U,\ \exists r>0:\ B(x;r)\subseteq U\}\text{ is a topology on }X

nodes.title: Equivalent Metrics Induce the Same Topology
nodes.originalText:
Let рЭСЛ be any set, and let рЭСС1 and рЭСС2 be two equivalent metrics on рЭСЛ. Then оЙАрЭСС1 = оЙАрЭСС2 .

14 3.1. Definition and examples

This follows from Exercise 2.7.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $X$ be a set and let $d_1$ and $d_2$ be equivalent metrics on $X$. Then the metric topologies agree: $\mathcal{T}_{d_1}=\mathcal{T}_{d_2}$.
nodes.explanation: Equivalent metrics have the same open sets, so they determine the same topology even if their numerical distances differ.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: d_1\sim d_2\Longrightarrow \mathcal{T}_{d_1}=\mathcal{T}_{d_2}

nodes.title: Discrete topology
nodes.originalText:
Let рЭСЛ be any set. The collection оЙА of all subsets of рЭСЛ, i.e. the power set оИЉ(рЭСЛ) of рЭСЛ, is a topology on рЭСЛ. We refer to this topology as the discrete topology. A set рЭСЛ equipped with the discrete topology is referred to as a discrete topological space.

The discrete topology is the unique topology where the singletons are open. We can think of a discrete topological space as a space of separate, isolated points, with no close interaction between different points. For any set рЭСЛ, the discrete topology is the largest topology we may equip рЭСЛ with. The smallest topology is called the indiscrete topology.
nodes.formalStatement: For any set $X$, the power set $\mathcal{P}(X)$ is a topology on $X$. This topology is called the discrete topology.
nodes.explanation: The discrete topology makes every subset open. It is the largest topology on a fixed set and models spaces whose points are completely separated.

nodes.title: Indiscrete topology
nodes.originalText: Let рЭСЛ be any set. The collection оЙА consisting of вИЕ and рЭСЛ is a topology on рЭСЛ, referred to as the indiscrete topology on рЭСЛ. A set рЭСЛ equipped with the indiscrete topology is referred to as an indiscrete topological space.
nodes.formalStatement: For any set $X$, the collection $\{\emptyset,X\}$ is a topology on $X$. This topology is called the indiscrete topology.
nodes.explanation: The indiscrete topology has only the two open sets required by the topology axioms. It is the smallest topology on a fixed set.

nodes.title: Topologies on a Three-Point Set
nodes.formalStatement: Let рЭСЛ = {рЭСО, рЭСП, рЭСР}. The following collections all define a topology on рЭСЛ. (1) оЙА1 = оЙАind = {вИЕ, рЭСЛ}
nodes.explanation: Illustrates topologies on a three-point set in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Comparable topologies
nodes.originalText:
Let рЭСЛ be any set and suppose that оЙА1 and оЙА2 are two topologies on рЭСЛ. If оЙА1 вКЖ оЙА2 , we say that оЙА1 is coarser than оЙА2 and that оЙА2 is finer than оЙА1 . We say that оЙА1 and оЙА2 are comparable if either оЙА1 вКЖ оЙА2 or оЙА2 вКЖ оЙА1 .

Clearly, for any set рЭСЛ, the discrete topology оЙАdisc contains the indiscrete topology оЙАind : оЙАdisc вКЗ оЙАind . Hence, the discrete topology is finer than the indiscrete topology and the indiscrete topology is coarser than the discrete topology.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $X$ be a set and let $\mathcal{T}_1,\mathcal{T}_2$ be topologies on $X$. If $\mathcal{T}_1\subseteq\mathcal{T}_2$, then $\mathcal{T}_1$ is coarser than $\mathcal{T}_2$ and $\mathcal{T}_2$ is finer than $\mathcal{T}_1$. The topologies are comparable if either one is contained in the other.
nodes.explanation: This definition orders topologies on a fixed set by inclusion of open sets. A finer topology has more open sets; a coarser topology has fewer.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: \mathcal{T}_1\preceq\mathcal{T}_2\iff \mathcal{T}_1\subseteq\mathcal{T}_2,\qquad \mathcal{T}_1,\mathcal{T}_2\text{ comparable}\iff \mathcal{T}_1\subseteq\mathcal{T}_2\text{ or }\mathcal{T}_2\subseteq\mathcal{T}_1

nodes.title: Cofinite topology
nodes.originalText:
Let рЭСЛ be any set. The collection оЙА of subsets of рЭСЛ consisting of subsets рЭСИ вКЖ рЭСЛ such that рЭСИ рЭСР = рЭСЛ вІµ рЭСИ is either finite or all of рЭСЛ is a topology on рЭСЛ. We refer to this topology as the cofinite topology on рЭСЛ.

Chapter 3. Topological spaces 15

If рЭСЛ is a finite set, the cofinite topology is equal to the discrete topology. If рЭСЛ is an infinite set, the cofinite topology is strictly coarser than the discrete topology, in the sense that the cofinite topology is properly contained in the discrete topology. We end this section with a theorem that we can use to prove that some set is open. To state the theorem we need the following definition.
nodes.formalStatement: For a set $X$, the collection of subsets $U\subseteq X$ such that $X\setminus U$ is finite or equal to all of $X$ is a topology on $X$, called the cofinite topology.
nodes.explanation: The cofinite topology is a standard example between the indiscrete and discrete topologies on an infinite set.

nodes.title: Neighborhoods
nodes.originalText:
Let рЭСЛ be a topological space, рЭСИ a subset of рЭСЛ and рЭС• вИИ рЭСЛ. We say that рЭСИ is a neighborhood of рЭС• if рЭС• вИИ рЭСИ and рЭСИ is open in рЭСЛ.

A neighborhood in the sense of the previous definition is sometimes referred to as an open neigh- borhood, cf. Definition 2.11.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $X$ be a topological space, let $U\subseteq X$, and let $x\in X$. The set $U$ is a neighborhood of $x$ if $x\in U$ and $U$ is open in $X$.
nodes.explanation: In a topological space, a neighborhood is simply an open set containing the point. This is the topological analogue of metric neighborhoods.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: U\text{ is a neighborhood of }x\iff x\in U\in\mathcal{T}

nodes.title: Open Sets via Neighborhoods
nodes.originalText:
Let рЭСЛ be a topological space. A subset рЭСИ of рЭСЛ is open in рЭСЛ if and only if for every рЭС• вИИ рЭСИ there is a neighborhood рЭСИрЭС• of рЭС• such that рЭСИрЭС• вКЖ рЭСИ .

Proof. Assume that рЭСИ is open in рЭСЛ. Then for every рЭС• вИИ рЭСИ , рЭСИ is a neighborhood of рЭС• that is contained in рЭСИ . We prove the other implication. Assume that for every рЭС• вИИ рЭСИ there is a рЭСИрЭС• вИИ оЙА such that рЭС• вИИ рЭСИрЭС• вКЖ рЭСИ , i.e., that рЭСИрЭС• is a neighborhood of рЭС• such that рЭСИрЭС• вКЖ рЭСИ . To prove that рЭСИ вИИ оЙА , we will вЛГ вЛГ prove that рЭСИ = рЭС•вИИрЭСИ рЭСИрЭС• . Assume that рЭС•вА≤ вИИ рЭСИрЭС•вА≤ . Then рЭС•вА≤ вИИ рЭСИрЭС•вА≤ вКЖ рЭС•вИИрЭСИ рЭСИрЭС• . Furthermore, any вЛГ point in рЭС•вИИрЭСИ рЭСИрЭС• is in рЭСИрЭС• for some рЭС• вИИ рЭСИ so by assumption, рЭСИрЭС• вКЖ рЭСИ and рЭС• вИИ рЭСИрЭС• вКЖ рЭСИ . Hence, вЛГ рЭСИ = рЭС•вИИрЭСИ рЭСИрЭС• . As рЭСИ is the union of open sets it must be an open set as well by T2.

3.2 Continuous maps We know from Theorem 2.13 that a map between metric spaces is continuous if and only if the preimage of an open set is open. This motivates the following definition.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $X$ be a topological space. A subset $U\subseteq X$ is open in $X$ if and only if for every $x\in U$ there exists a neighborhood $U_x$ of $x$ such that $U_x\subseteq U$.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: U\subseteq X\text{ open}\iff \forall x\in U,\ \exists U_x\in\mathcal{T}:\ x\in U_x\subseteq U
nodes.explanation: This theorem characterizes open sets pointwise by neighborhoods. The proof uses the fact that an arbitrary union of open neighborhoods is open.

nodes.title: Continuous maps between topological spaces
nodes.originalText:
Let рЭСЛ and рЭСМ be topological spaces. A map рЭСУ вИґ рЭСЛ вЖТ рЭСМ is said to be continuous if preimages of open sets are open, i.e., if рЭСЙ is an open set in рЭСМ then the preimage рЭСУ вИТ1 (рЭСЙ ) of рЭСЙ under рЭСУ is open in рЭСЛ.

Hence, all continuous maps between metric spaces (рЭСЛ, рЭССрЭСЛ ) and (рЭСМ , рЭССрЭСМ ) are also continuous maps between the corresponding topological spaces рЭСЛ and рЭСМ with the metric topologies оЙАрЭССрЭСЛ and оЙАрЭССрЭСМ , re- spectively.
nodes.formalStatement: Let $X$ and $Y$ be topological spaces. A map $f:X\to Y$ is continuous if for every open set $V\subseteq Y$, the preimage $f^{-1}(V)$ is open in $X$.
nodes.explanation: Topological continuity is defined by the preimage of open sets. This definition is motivated by the metric preimage criterion and no longer requires distances.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: f:X\to Y\text{ is continuous}\iff \forall V\in\mathcal{T}_Y,\ f^{-1}(V)\in\mathcal{T}_X

nodes.title: Constant Maps Are Continuous
nodes.originalText: Let рЭСЛ and рЭСМ be topological spaces. Then all constant maps from рЭСЛ to рЭСМ are continuous: the preimages are either empty or the entire space, and these are always open, cf. T1.
nodes.formalStatement: Let рЭСЛ and рЭСМ be topological spaces. Then all constant maps from рЭСЛ to рЭСМ are continuous: the preimages are either empty or the entire space, and these are always open, cf. T1.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates constant maps are continuous in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Maps from Discrete Spaces Are Continuous
nodes.originalText: Let рЭСЛ be a discrete topological space and рЭСМ a topological space. Then all maps from рЭСЛ to рЭСМ are continuous.
nodes.formalStatement: Let рЭСЛ be a discrete topological space and рЭСМ a topological space. Then all maps from рЭСЛ to рЭСМ are continuous.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates maps from discrete spaces are continuous in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Maps into Indiscrete Spaces Are Continuous
nodes.originalText:
Let рЭСЛ be any topological space and рЭСМ be an indiscrete topological space. Then all maps from рЭСЛ to рЭСМ are continuous.

16 3.2. Continuous maps
nodes.formalStatement: Let рЭСЛ be any topological space and рЭСМ be an indiscrete topological space. Then all maps from рЭСЛ to рЭСМ are continuous.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates maps into indiscrete spaces are continuous in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Identity Maps Between Discrete and Standard Real Lines
nodes.originalText:
Let вДЭdisc be the discrete topological space consisting of the real numbers with the discrete topology, and let вДЭ be the topological space consisting of the the real numbers with the usual (Euclidean) metric topology. Then the identity map id вДЭdisc вЖРвЖРвЖРвЖРвЖТ вДЭ

is continuous by Example 3.13, while the identity map id вДЭ вЖРвЖРвЖРвЖРвЖТ вДЭdisc

is not continuous: singletons are open in the discrete topology but not in the (Euclidean) metric topology.

The following theorem says that the composition of two continuous maps is a continuous map.
nodes.formalStatement: Let вДЭdisc be the discrete topological space consisting of the real numbers with the discrete topology, and let вДЭ be the topological space consisting of the the real numbers with the usual (Euclidean) metric topology. Then the identity map id вДЭdisc вЖРвЖРвЖРвЖРвЖТ вДЭ is continuous by Example 3.13, while the identity map id вДЭ вЖРвЖРвЖРвЖРвЖТ вДЭdisc
nodes.explanation: Illustrates identity maps between discrete and standard real lines in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Composition of Continuous Maps
nodes.originalText:
Let рЭСЛ, рЭСМ and рЭСН be topological spaces. If рЭСУ вИґ рЭСЛ вЖТ рЭСМ and рЭСФ вИґ рЭСМ вЖТ рЭСН are continuous maps, then the composite рЭСФвЧ¶рЭСУ вИґ рЭСЛ вЖТ рЭСН is continuous.

Proof. Let рЭСК вКЖ рЭСН be open in рЭСН. We need to prove that (рЭСФвЧ¶рЭСУ )вИТ1 (рЭСК ) is open in рЭСЛ. Since

(рЭСФвЧ¶рЭСУ )вИТ1 (рЭСК ) = {рЭС• вИИ рЭСЛ вИ£ рЭСФ(рЭСУ (рЭС•)) вИИ рЭСК } = {рЭС• вИИ рЭСЛ вИ£ рЭСУ (рЭС•) вИИ рЭСФ вИТ1 (рЭСК )} = {рЭС• вИИ рЭСЛ вИ£ рЭС• вИИ рЭСУ вИТ1 (рЭСФ вИТ1 (рЭСК ))} = рЭСУ вИТ1 (рЭСФ вИТ1 (рЭСК ))

and that рЭСФ вИТ1 (рЭСК ) is open in рЭСМ and рЭСУ вИТ1 (рЭСФ вИТ1 (рЭСК )) is open in рЭСЛ (by continuity of рЭСФ and рЭСУ ), it follows that (рЭСФвЧ¶рЭСУ )вИТ1 (рЭСК ) is open in рЭСЛ. Hence, рЭСФвЧ¶рЭСУ is continuous.

We can express continuity at a point for maps between topological spaces using neighborhoods. (See Theorem 2.12 for the case of metric spaces.)
nodes.formalStatement: Let $X$, $Y$, and $Z$ be topological spaces. If $f:X\to Y$ and $g:Y\to Z$ are continuous maps, then the composite $g\circ f:X\to Z$ is continuous.
nodes.explanation: Continuous maps are closed under composition. The proof uses the identity $(g\circ f)^{-1}(W)=f^{-1}(g^{-1}(W))$ for an open set $W\subseteq Z$.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: f:X\to Y,\ g:Y\to Z\text{ continuous}\Longrightarrow g\circ f:X\to Z\text{ continuous}

nodes.title: Continuity at a point
nodes.originalText: Let рЭСЛ and рЭСМ be topological spaces, and let рЭС• вИИ рЭСЛ. A map рЭСУ вИґ рЭСЛ вЖТ рЭСМ is continuous at рЭС• if for all neighborhoods рЭСЙ of рЭСУ (рЭС•) there is a neighborhood рЭСИ of рЭС• such that рЭСУ (рЭСИ ) вКЖ рЭСЙ .
nodes.formalStatement: Let рЭСЛ and рЭСМ be topological spaces, and let рЭС• вИИ рЭСЛ. A map рЭСУ вИґ рЭСЛ вЖТ рЭСМ is continuous at рЭС• if for all neighborhoods рЭСЙ of рЭСУ (рЭС•) there is a neighborhood рЭСИ of рЭС• such that рЭСУ (рЭСИ ) вКЖ рЭСЙ .
nodes.explanation: Defines continuity at a point and fixes terminology used later in continuity, maps, topology.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: f\text{ is continuous at }x\iff \forall V\ni f(x)\text{ open},\ \exists U\ni x\text{ open such that }f(U)\subseteq V

nodes.title: Continuity Is Pointwise Continuity
nodes.originalText:
Let рЭСЛ and рЭСМ be topological spaces. A map рЭСУ вИґ рЭСЛ вЖТ рЭСМ is continuous if and only if it is continuous at each рЭС• вИИ рЭСЛ.

Proof. Assume that рЭСУ is continuous, and let рЭС• вИИ рЭСЛ and рЭСЙ be a neighborhood of рЭСУ (рЭС•). Then the set рЭСИ = рЭСУ вИТ1 (рЭСЙ ) is a neighborhood of рЭС• such that рЭСУ (рЭСИ ) вКЖ рЭСЙ . Assume that рЭСУ is continuous at each рЭС• вИИ рЭСЛ. Let рЭСЙ вКЖ рЭСМ be open in рЭСМ . Choose рЭС• вИИ рЭСУ вИТ1 (рЭСЙ ). Since рЭСУ is continuous at рЭС• there is neighborhood рЭСИрЭС• of рЭС• such that рЭСУ (рЭСИрЭС• ) вКЖ рЭСЙ . Hence, рЭСИрЭС• вКЖ рЭСУ вИТ1 (рЭСЙ ). It follows that рЭСУ вИТ1 (рЭСЙ ) can be written as the union of the open sets рЭСИрЭС• , and hence, it is open in рЭСЛ. Thus рЭСУ is continuous.

Chapter 3. Topological spaces 17

3.3 Homeomorphisms We now introduce the notion of topological equivalence, also known as homeomorphism.
nodes.formalStatement: Let рЭСЛ and рЭСМ be topological spaces. A map рЭСУ вИґ рЭСЛ вЖТ рЭСМ is continuous if and only if it is continuous at each рЭС• вИИ рЭСЛ.
nodes.explanation: States a main result about continuity is pointwise continuity; it is used as a dependency or milestone in the surrounding development.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: f:X\to Y\text{ continuous}\iff \forall x\in X,\ f\text{ is continuous at }x

nodes.title: Homeomorphisms
nodes.originalText:
Let рЭСЛ and рЭСМ be topological spaces. A bijective map рЭСУ вИґ рЭСЛ вЖТ рЭСМ with the property that both рЭСУ and рЭСУ вИТ1 вИґ рЭСМ вЖТ рЭСЛ are continuous, is called a homeo- morphism. If there exists a homeomorphism рЭСУ вИґ рЭСЛ вЖТ рЭСМ , we say that рЭСЛ and рЭСМ are homeomor- phic and write рЭСЛ вЙЕ рЭСМ .

A homeomorphism рЭСУ вИґ рЭСЛ вЖТ рЭСМ gives a one-to-one correspondence between open sets in рЭСЛ and рЭСМ . As a result, any property of a topological space that can be expressed in terms of its elements and its open subsets is preserved by homeomorphisms. Such a property is called a topological property.
nodes.formalStatement: Let рЭСЛ and рЭСМ be topological spaces. A bijective map рЭСУ вИґ рЭСЛ вЖТ рЭСМ with the property that both рЭСУ and рЭСУ вИТ1 вИґ рЭСМ вЖТ рЭСЛ are continuous, is called a homeomorphism. If there exists a homeomorphism рЭСУ вИґ рЭСЛ вЖТ рЭСМ , we say that рЭСЛ and рЭСМ are homeomorphic and write рЭСЛ вЙЕ рЭСМ . A homeomorphism рЭСУ вИґ рЭСЛ вЖТ рЭСМ gives a one-to-one correspondence between open sets in рЭСЛ and рЭСМ . As a result, any property of a topological space that can be expressed in terms of its elements and its open subsets is preserved by homeomorphisms. Such a property is called a topological property.
nodes.explanation: Defines homeomorphisms and fixes terminology used later in continuity, homeomorphism, maps.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: X\cong Y\iff \exists f:X\to Y\text{ bijective such that }f\text{ and }f^{-1}\text{ are continuous}

nodes.title: Affine Homeomorphism of the Real Line
nodes.originalText:
Let вДЭ be the topological space of the real numbers with the (Euclidean) metric topology. The map рЭСУ вИґ вДЭ вЖТ вДЭ given by

рЭСУ (рЭС•) = 2рЭС• вИТ 1

is a homeomorphism. Let рЭСФ вИґ вДЭ вЖТ вДЭ be given by

рЭСФ(рЭС¶) = (рЭС¶ + 1) then, clearly, рЭСФ(рЭСУ (рЭС•)) = рЭС• and рЭСУ (рЭСФ(рЭС¶)) = рЭС¶ for all real numbers рЭС• and рЭС¶. Thus рЭСУ is a bijection and рЭСУ вИТ1 = рЭСФ. From calculus we know that рЭСУ and рЭСФ are continuous. Hence, рЭСУ is a homeomorphism.
nodes.formalStatement: Let вДЭ be the topological space of the real numbers with the (Euclidean) metric topology. The map рЭСУ вИґ вДЭ вЖТ вДЭ given by рЭСУ (рЭС•) = 2рЭС• вИТ 1
nodes.explanation: Illustrates affine homeomorphism of the real line in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Continuous Bijection Between Nonidentical Two-Point Topologies
nodes.originalText:
Let рЭСЛ = {рЭСО, рЭСП}, and let оЙА1 = {вИЕ, {рЭСО}, рЭСЛ} and оЙА2 = {вИЕ, {рЭСП}, рЭСЛ} be two topolo- gies on рЭСЛ. The map рЭСУ вИґ рЭСЛ вЖТ рЭСЛ given by рЭСУ (рЭСО) = рЭСП and рЭСУ (рЭСП) = рЭСО is clearly a continuous bijection (with the domain given оЙА1 as topology, and the codomain given оЙА2 as topology). Also, рЭСУ is its own inverse: рЭСУ = рЭСУ вИТ1 . Hence, рЭСУ is a homeomorphism and (рЭСЛ, оЙА1 ) вЙЕ (рЭСЛ, оЙА2 ).

Homeomorphisms are continuous bijections, but the converse is not true.
nodes.formalStatement: Let рЭСЛ = {рЭСО, рЭСП}, and let оЙА1 = {вИЕ, {рЭСО}, рЭСЛ} and оЙА2 = {вИЕ, {рЭСП}, рЭСЛ} be two topologies on рЭСЛ. The map рЭСУ вИґ рЭСЛ вЖТ рЭСЛ given by рЭСУ (рЭСО) = рЭСП and рЭСУ (рЭСП) = рЭСО is clearly a continuous bijection (with the domain given оЙА1 as topology, and the codomain given оЙА2 as topology). Also, рЭСУ is its own inverse: рЭСУ = рЭСУ вИТ1 . Hence, рЭСУ is a homeomorphism and (рЭСЛ, оЙА1 ) вЙЕ (рЭСЛ, оЙА2 ). Homeomorphisms are continuous bijections, but the converse is not true.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates continuous bijection between nonidentical two-point topologies in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Continuous Bijection Need Not Be a Homeomorphism
nodes.originalText:
Let рЭСЛ = {рЭСО, рЭСП}. The identity map id вИґ рЭСЛ вЖТ рЭСЛ where the domain is given the discrete topology and the codomain is given the indiscrete topology is a continuous bijection but not a homeomorphism: the inverse map is not continuous.

The following theorem says that being homeomorphic is an equivalence relation on any set of topological spaces.
nodes.formalStatement: Let рЭСЛ = {рЭСО, рЭСП}. The identity map id вИґ рЭСЛ вЖТ рЭСЛ where the domain is given the discrete topology and the codomain is given the indiscrete topology is a continuous bijection but not a homeomorphism: the inverse map is not continuous.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates continuous bijection need not be a homeomorphism in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Homeomorphism Is an Equivalence Relation
nodes.originalText:
Let рЭСЛ, рЭСМ and рЭСН be topological spaces.

Reflexivity The identity map id вИґ рЭСЛ вЖТ рЭСЛ (where the domain and the codomain are equipped with the same topology), given by id(рЭС•) = рЭС• for рЭС• вИИ рЭСЛ, is a homeomorphism.

Symmetry If рЭСУ вИґ рЭСЛ вЖТ рЭСМ is a homeomorphism, then рЭСУ вИТ1 вИґ рЭСМ вЖТ рЭСЛ is also a homeomor- phism.

Transitivity If рЭСУ вИґ рЭСЛ вЖТ рЭСМ and рЭСФ вИґ рЭСМ вЖТ рЭСН are homeomorphisms, then рЭСФвЧ¶рЭСУ вИґ рЭСЛ вЖТ рЭСН is also

18 3.4. Closed sets

a homeomorphism.

Proof. The identity map id вИґ рЭСЛ вЖТ рЭСЛ (where the domain and the codomain are equipped with the same topology) is clearly continuous and bijective. As the identity map is its own inverse, then it is also a homeomorphism. Hence, рЭСЛ вЙЕ рЭСЛ and so вЙЕ satisfies the reflexivity condition for an equivalence relation. If рЭСУ вИґ рЭСЛ вЖТ рЭСМ is a homeomorphism, then рЭСУ вИТ1 вИґ рЭСМ вЖТ рЭСЛ is also a homeomorphism: рЭСУ вИТ1 is a continuous bijection with continuous inverse (рЭСУ вИТ1 )вИТ1 = рЭСУ вИґ рЭСЛ вЖТ рЭСМ . Hence, рЭСЛ вЙЕ рЭСМ if and only if рЭСМ вЙЕ рЭСЛ. Thus вЙЕ satisfies the symmetry condition for an equivalence relation.

Theorem 3.16 tells us that the composition of two homeomorphisms рЭСУ вИґ рЭСЛ вЖТ рЭСМ and рЭСФ вИґ рЭСМ вЖТ рЭСН is continuous. The composition of two bijective maps is always bijective. Hence, рЭСФвЧ¶рЭСУ is a continuous bijection. We need to prove that its inverse, (рЭСФвЧ¶рЭСУ )вИТ1 , is continuous. Since

(рЭСФвЧ¶рЭСУ )вИТ1 = рЭСУ вИТ1 вЧ¶рЭСФ вИТ1

is a composition of continuous maps, then by Theorem 3.16 so is (рЭСФвЧ¶рЭСУ )вИТ1 . Thus рЭСФвЧ¶рЭСУ is a homeomorphism. Hence, if рЭСЛ вЙЕ рЭСМ and рЭСМ вЙЕ рЭСН, then рЭСЛ вЙЕ рЭСН. Thus вЙЕ satisfies the transitivity condition for an equivalence relation.
nodes.formalStatement: For topological spaces $X$, $Y$, and $Z$, being homeomorphic is an equivalence relation: $X\cong X$; if $X\cong Y$ then $Y\cong X$; and if $X\cong Y$ and $Y\cong Z$ then $X\cong Z$.
nodes.explanation: This theorem formalizes the idea that homeomorphic spaces are topologically the same. The proof checks reflexivity by the identity map, symmetry by taking inverses, and transitivity by composing homeomorphisms.

mergedFrom.reason: This was extracted as a duplicate theorem 3.16, but its text is the transitivity proof continuation for Theorem 3.23.
mergedFrom.cleanText:
Theorem 3.16 tells us that the composition of two homeomorphisms рЭСУ вИґ рЭСЛ вЖТ рЭСМ and рЭСФ вИґ рЭСМ вЖТ рЭСН is continuous. The composition of two bijective maps is always bijective. Hence, рЭСФвЧ¶рЭСУ is a continuous bijection. We need to prove that its inverse, (рЭСФвЧ¶рЭСУ )вИТ1 , is continuous. Since

(рЭСФвЧ¶рЭСУ )вИТ1 = рЭСУ вИТ1 вЧ¶рЭСФ вИТ1

is a composition of continuous maps, then by Theorem 3.16 so is (рЭСФвЧ¶рЭСУ )вИТ1 . Thus рЭСФвЧ¶рЭСУ is a homeomorphism. Hence, if рЭСЛ вЙЕ рЭСМ and рЭСМ вЙЕ рЭСН, then рЭСЛ вЙЕ рЭСН. Thus вЙЕ satisfies the transitivity condition for an equivalence relation.

nodes.mathematicalFormula: X\cong X,\quad X\cong Y\Rightarrow Y\cong X,\quad (X\cong Y\land Y\cong Z)\Rightarrow X\cong Z

nodes.title: Closed subsets
nodes.originalText:
A subset рЭРЊ of a topological space рЭСЛ is closed in рЭСЛ if and only if the complement рЭРЊ рЭСР = рЭСЛ вІµ рЭРЊ is open in рЭСЛ.

This is completely analogous to how we defined closed subsets in metric spaces, cf. Definition 2.9.
nodes.formalStatement: A subset $K$ of a topological space $X$ is closed in $X$ if and only if its complement $X\setminus K$ is open in $X$.
nodes.explanation: Defines closed subsets and fixes terminology used later in closed-sets, metric, open-sets.
nodes.mathematicalFormula: K\subseteq X\text{ is closed}\iff X\setminus K\in\mathcal{T}

nodes.title: Closed Sets in a Discrete Space
nodes.originalText: Let рЭСЛ be a discrete topological space. Since every subset of рЭСЛ is open in рЭСЛ, it follows that every subset of рЭСЛ is also closed in рЭСЛ.
nodes.formalStatement: Let рЭСЛ be a discrete topological space. Since every subset of рЭСЛ is open in рЭСЛ, it follows that every subset of рЭСЛ is also closed in рЭСЛ.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates closed sets in a discrete space in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Closed Sets in an Indiscrete Space
nodes.originalText:
Let рЭСЛ be an indiscrete topological space. The only subsets of рЭСЛ that are closed in рЭСЛ are вИЕ and рЭСЛ (which are also the only subsets that are open in рЭСЛ).

Recall that in a discrete topological space, all the singletons are open sets. This is usually not the case.
nodes.formalStatement: Let рЭСЛ be an indiscrete topological space. The only subsets of рЭСЛ that are closed in рЭСЛ are вИЕ and рЭСЛ (which are also the only subsets that are open in рЭСЛ). Recall that in a discrete topological space, all the singletons are open sets. This is usually not the case.
nodes.explanation: Illustrates closed sets in an indiscrete space in a concrete case, helping connect the definitions and theorems to examples.

nodes.title: Closed Intervals in the Real Line
nodes.originalText:
Let вДЭ be the topological space of the real numbers with the (Euclidean) metric topology. Then every subset [рЭСО, рЭСП] = {рЭС• вИИ вДЭ вИ£ рЭСО в©љ рЭС• в©љ рЭСП} вКЖ вДЭ is closed in вДЭ: the complement [рЭСО, рЭСП]рЭСР = вДЭ вІµ [рЭСО, рЭСП] = (вИТвИЮ, рЭСО) вИ™ (рЭСП, вИЮ) is a union of open sets in вДЭ, and hence, is open in вДЭ. Furthermore, all the singletons are closed: the complement {рЭСО}рЭСР = вДЭ вІµ {рЭСО} = (вИТвИЮ, рЭСО) вИ™ (рЭСО, вИЮ) is a union of open sets in вДЭ, and hence, is open in вДЭ.

By passing to complements we get the following theorem.
